straight_beat.name: Raymond 'Ray' Tortorelli
straight_beat.column:
The Yankees lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to rip the scoreboard off the wall. The Angels handed them a 7-1 beatdown yesterday, and now they’re 9-8, 0.5 games behind the Rays in the AL East. It’s the kind of day that makes you think about the old days—when the Bronx box was a place where teams came to die, not just visit.  

You remember the 1996 team, right? The one that turned the Bronx box into a fortress. They’d walk you through the gates, let you sit in the stands, and then boom—here comes the magic. Jeter’s flip play, the way the offense would ignite like a matchstick in a hurricane. That’s the standard we’re supposed to measure by. But lately, the Yankees have been playing like they’re trying to hit a home run with a cue ball.  

The offense? They’re still relying on Judge to carry the load. And sure, he’s hitting .310 with

optimist.name: Franca 'Frannie' Bellino
optimist.column:
The Yankees lost yesterday, but that’s just a footnote in the story of a team that’s still got the taste of a parade in its mouth. You know what I saw from Juan Soto yesterday? October in his eyes. By the time the leaves turn we’re having a parade, and I’m catering it.  

Let’s be real—this team isn’t about to fold. They’re young, hungry, and packed with talent that’s still finding its groove. Sure, the Angels handed them a 7-1 drubbing, but that’s the kind of game you hit a reset button on. You can’t measure a team by one day’s performance, especially when the calendar’s still in September. The Yankees are building something, and sometimes building takes time. Think of it like a pastrami on rye—fresh, smoky, and getting better with every bite.  

The front office has been busy, and not just with trades. They’re laying the groundwork for a future that’s gonna be sweet. You see that? That’s the kind of patience that turns a deli into a landmark. Arthur Avenue’s been around longer than most of these players, and it’s still standing because it knows how to wait for the right moment.  

Now, I won’t sugarcoat it—the Yankees are in a tight

pessimist.name: Sol Hirschberg
pessimist.column:
The Yankees lost again. Not in a way that feels inevitable, but in a way that makes you want to throw your television out the window. Seven to one. The Angels, of all teams, did the damage. You’d think with the payroll they’ve got, they’d have a better idea of how to win. But no, they’re just another team in a league full of them. And here we are, 9-8, half a game behind the Rays, a team that’s been in the playoffs for the last three years. It’s not just the record that’s haunting— it’s the way the Bombers keep clawing their way back into contention only to slip again, like a drunk on a tightrope.  

Cashman’s got a plan, sure. He’s handed out extensions like Halloween candy, but where’s the payoff? The front office is doing the same thing they did in 2009, when they signed CC Sabathia and Mark Teixeira and thought they’d magically turn into a contender. Except this time, the math doesn’t add up. The Yankees have the pieces, but they’re not clicking.
